file_name: Apollo Endosurgery - Manufacturing and Supply Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: ESTABLISHMENT shall manufacture the Product in accordance with the Specifications of this Agreement, and shall comply with all quality system requirement communicated by Apollo from time to time, ISO 13485:2012 and any applicable cGMPs and all other applicable local, United States or European regulations or laws in connection with the manufacture, testing, packaging, labeling, shipping, and handling of the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: ESTABLISHMENT shall endeavor to manufacture the Product in general accordance with the Specifications of this Agreement, and shall consider all quality system requirements communicated by Apollo from time to time, ISO 13485:2012 and applicable cGMPs and other local, United States or European regulations or laws in connection with the manufacture, testing, packaging, labeling, shipping, and handling of the Product.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall manufacture' with 'shall endeavor to manufacture' and 'shall comply' with 'shall consider' weakens the obligation to strictly adhere to specifications, quality system requirements, and regulations. Adding the word 'general' before accordance also introduces ambiguity. This implies that strict adherence is not mandatory, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.70 mandates that manufacturers 'shall' establish and maintain procedures for identifying product during all stages of receiving, production, distribution, and installation to prevent mix-ups and ensure proper control. The modified text, by using 'endeavor to manufacture' and 'consider', contradicts this mandatory requirement by implying that adherence to specifications and regulatory compliance is discretionary.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to strictly adhere to manufacturing specifications and quality system requirements. By changing 'shall manufacture' to 'shall endeavor to manufacture' and 'shall comply' to 'shall consider', the original contract's mandatory obligations become discretionary. This contradicts 21 CFR § 820.70, as explained in the Law Explanation, which mandates that manufacturers 'shall' establish and maintain procedures to ensure proper control. The original text ensured compliance with these procedures through its explicit 'shall' statements, while the modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to non-compliance, as highlighted by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: ESTABLISHMENT agrees that its work under this Agreement will be conducted in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, and with the standard of care customary in the industry.
perturbation.changed_text: ESTABLISHMENT agrees that its work under this Agreement will be conducted with an attempt to comply with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, and with reasonable care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'in compliance' to 'with an attempt to comply' introduces ambiguity regarding the level of commitment to following laws and regulations. Similarly, replacing 'the standard of care customary in the industry' with 'reasonable care' creates a vague standard, open to subjective interpretation. This could lead to a lower standard of performance than what's typically expected in the medical device manufacturing industry.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.20 requires that management with executive responsibility shall establish policy and objectives for, and commitment to, quality. The ambiguous phrasing 'attempt to comply' undermines this requirement by suggesting a lack of firm commitment to compliance, potentially falling short of the FDA's expectation for rigorous quality management systems.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 820.20 - Management responsibility. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 820.20 - Management responsibility. CFR prev | next § 820.20 Management responsibility. (a) Quality policy. Management with executive responsibility shall establish its policy and objectives for, and commitment to, quality . Management with executive responsibility shall ensure that the quality policy is understood, implemented, and maintained at all levels of the organization. (b) Organization. Each manufacturer shall establish and maintain an adequate organizational structure to ensure that devices are designed and produced in accordance with the requirements of this part. (1) Responsibility and authority. Each manufacturer shall establish the appropriate responsibility, authority, and interrelation of all personnel who manage, perform, and assess work affecting quality , and provide the independence and authority necessary to perform these tasks. (2) Resources. Each manufacturer shall provide adequate resources, including the assignment of trained personnel, for management, performance of work, and assessment activities, including internal quality audits, to meet the requirements of this part. (3) Management representative. Management with executive responsibility shall appoint, and document such appointment of, a member of management who, irrespective of other responsibilities, shall have established authority over and responsibility for: (i) Ensuring that quality system requirements are effectively established and effectively maintained in accordance with this part; and (ii) Reporting on the performance of the quality system to management with executive responsibility for review. (c) Management review. Management with executive responsibility shall review the suitability and effectiveness of the quality system at defined intervals and with sufficient frequency according to establishe
perturbation.justification: The change from "in compliance" to "with an attempt to comply" significantly weakens the commitment to adhering to laws and regulations. This contradicts 21 CFR § 820.20, which mandates that management establish policy and objectives for, and commitment to, quality. The original text ensures a firm commitment, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and suggests a potentially lower standard of compliance. Additionally, the change from "the standard of care customary in the industry" to "reasonable care" introduces a subjective standard, which does not align with the need for adequate resources, including the assignment of trained personnel, for management, performance of work, and assessment activities, to meet the requirements of this part, as outlined in 21 CFR § 820.20 (b)(2). The ambiguity created can be contested in court. Since the change undermines the clear legal requirement for a robust quality management system, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: ESTABLISHMENT shall permit APOLLO and its agents, during business hours and upon notice to ESTABLISHMENT, to inspect the Facilities where the Product is manufactured, handled, stored or tested, as well as all processes relating to the manufacture, handling, storage, or testing of the Product, as well as all test records regarding the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: ESTABLISHMENT shall generally permit APOLLO and its agents, during standard business hours and after reasonable notice to ESTABLISHMENT, to conduct tours of the Facilities where the Product is generally manufactured, handled, stored or tested, in order to get a glimpse of processes relating to the manufacture, handling, storage, or testing of the Product, as well as obtain summaries of test records regarding the Product.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'inspect' to 'conduct tours' and adding qualifiers like 'generally', 'standard business hours', 'reasonable notice', 'get a glimpse', and 'summaries of test records,' the original straightforward right to inspect becomes significantly weakened and less defined. The term 'generally' qualifies 'manufactured, handled, stored, or tested,' while the phrase 'get a glimpse' reduces the extent of the inspection to a brief overview rather than a thorough examination. Furthermore, providing only 'summaries of test records' limits APOLLO's access to essential data, hindering a comprehensive assessment of product quality and compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.180(a) requires that each manufacturer shall maintain device master record (DMRs). 820.180(c) requires that each manufacturer shall maintain device history records (DHRs). The regulation implies that these records must be complete and accessible for FDA review or audit. By limiting access to 'summaries of test records', the modified text obstructs a full and transparent review of records, potentially violating the FDA's requirement for comprehensive documentation and traceability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 820.180 - General requirements. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 820.180 - General requirements. CFR prev | next § 820.180 General requirements. All records required by this part shall be maintained at the manufacturing establishment or other location that is reasonably accessible to responsible officials of the manufacturer and to employees of FDA designated to perform inspections. Such records, including those not stored at the inspected establishment, shall be made readily available for review and copying by FDA employee(s). Such records shall be legible and shall be stored to minimize deterioration and to prevent loss. Those records stored in automated data processing systems shall be backed up. (a) Confidentiality. Records deemed confidential by the manufacturer may be marked to aid FDA in determining whether information may be disclosed under the public information regulation in part 20 of this chapter. (b) Record retention period. All records required by this part shall be retained for a period of time equivalent to the design and expected life of the device, but in no case less than 2 years from the date of release for commercial distribution by the manufacturer . (c) Exceptions. This section does not apply to the reports required by § 820.20(c) Management review, § 820.22 Quality audits, and supplier audit reports used to meet the requirements of § 820.50(a) Evaluation of suppliers, contractors, and consultants, but does apply to procedures established under these provisions. Upon request of a designated employee of FDA, an employee in management with executive responsibility shall certify in writing that the management reviews and quality audits required under this part, and supplier audits where applicable, have been performed and documented, the dates on which they were performed, and that any required corrective action has been undertaken. CFR
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens APOLLO's right to inspect the facility and obtain test records, potentially contradicting 21 CFR § 820.180, which mandates the maintenance of complete and accessible device master records (DMRs) and device history records (DHRs). By changing 'inspect' to 'conduct tours', limiting access to 'summaries of test records', and introducing qualifiers like 'generally', the revised text obstructs a full and transparent review of the facilities and documentation, violating the FDA's requirement for comprehensive documentation and traceability.
